content:
Hi! I'm new to submitting patches to the linux kernel so I thought I'd tackle issue #1123 to get familiar with the process.
I'm unsure whether the commit (or maybe it should be multiple commits?) for this issue should mention more than what is essentially just a "fix the clippy lints". Does this suffice or should I go into detail about something specific?

I'm also unsure if submitting the patch with open questions like these is deemed okay or if prior discussion is wanted to avoid noise on the ML. Here's the commit I'll be submitting: https://git.sr.ht/~witcher/linux/commit/fb0366c35ccd2119725b40e63c5a0e87fc55fa34

I'm unsure whether the commit (or maybe it should be multiple commits?) for this issue should mention more than what is essentially just a "fix the clippy lints"

It should typically explain more, especially for cases where it may not be clear why how to clean it up, i.e. one may apply Clippy's suggestion, or may #[allow] it, or may #[expect] it, or perhaps allow the lint at a module level, or maybe even disable globally the lint if we really do not want it anymore -- but why did you pick the option you picked? That is what the commit should explain.

You can even do one fix per commit, if you feel that is clearer for a reviewer. One per kind of lint is usually useful, even if it fixes several instances of the same lint, to separate discussions.

For instance, please take a look at these examples:

Commit 05cef2c4a421 ("rust: kunit: use C-string literals to clean warning")
Commit 7bc186731e87 ("rust: error: allow useless_conversion for 32-bit builds")
Commit 935c4833426f ("rust: rbtree: fix SAFETY comments that should be # Safety sections")

It may also be useful to include the clippy warning in the commit message. It's not necessary in every case, but if I saw your patch, I'd want to know why clippy is unhappy with (x < 8 && y < 8) || (x < 8 && y >= end) || (x >= end && y < 8)

I'm also unsure if submitting the patch with open questions like these is deemed okay or if prior discussion is wanted to avoid noise on the ML.

If you have a patch ready, and you feel it may require some discussion to understand whether it is a good or bad idea, you can prefix the patch with RFC (git format-patch --rfc), for instance. Discussing here in Zulip is another option too.

Alice Ryhl said:

It may also be useful to include the clippy warning in the commit message. It's not necessary in every case, but if I saw your patch, I'd want to know why clippy is unhappy with (x < 8 && y < 8) || (x < 8 && y >= end) || (x >= end && y < 8)

Yeah, that one (and the if version < 1 || version > 40 { one) are two that may generate some discussion :)
It may also be worth to compare v1 and v2 of this patch. Notice the review comments that I got on the commit message of v1 of the patch.

On 8/9/24 12:42 AM, Alice Ryhl wrote:

Dash has ascii value 45 and underscore has ascii value 95, so to correctly sort the includes, the underscore should be last.

This commit message lacks an explanation for why the change is being done. Yes it states that it brings the headers in ascii sort order, but WHY?
Thanks for the detailed explanations and examples! I'll rework the commit message and probably split the changes into more commits.

I'm commenting here because this concerns the same patches: The patches are ready to be sent to the mailing list. Following https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-patches, I'd send the patches to quite a lot of people already (i.e. the Maintainers, Reviewers and the rust-for-linux list mentioned in the MAINTAINERS file). I imagine I'd also need to send it to the maintainers for drivers/gpu/drm, which would make that quite a bit more. This also includes the patches being sent to both the rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org and linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org mailing lists.

Before I send the patches and annoy a bunch of people: Is this correct? Posting it like that would send the patches to 17 addresses, so I'd rather ask before I hit send.

That is correct, even if it sounds like a lot! :)

Please don't forget the DRM mailing list too and the author of the file too, i.e. of the QR code support.

I would also double-check the output of scripts/get_maintainer.pl.

I suppose the DRM mailing list would be dri-devel@lists.freedesktop.org? It's an educated guess from the MAINTAINERS file and output by scripts/get_maintainer.pl, is it referenced elsewhere that might be more obvious?

Also, is there a different way to figuring out who authored a piece of code besides looking at the git log?

And out of pure curiosity: I imagine scripts/get_maintainer.pl is parsing MAINTAINERS and has been used for quite a long time. Why the need to always double check specifically that?

Yes, lists/maintainers/reviewers should be taken from MAINTAINERS, and authors from Git (at least post-Git). scripts/get_maintainer.pl parses MAINTAINERS (and can also use Git), and may or may not show you things that you may not have considered, such as keyword matches (its behavior can be tweaked, too, see --help).

I just sent the patches to hopefully all the correct people and lists. Thanks a lot for the help!
https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241012075312.16342-1-witcher@wiredspace.de/T/#t
